past_topics: The etymology of debugging and why we call software problems "bugs
The word "algorithm" and its journey from a 9th-century Persian mathematician's name
The forgotten art of memory palaces and how ancient orators stored entire speeches in imaginary buildings
The cathedral builders' approach to software architecture: lessons from medieval masons who built for centuries
The word "sincere" and its forgotten connection to Roman sculptors who worked "without wax
The linguistic archaeology of "pixel" - from Latin points of light to digital dust
past_reflections: Writing about the evolution of 'bugs' from literal insects to digital metaphors made me realize how much our technical language carries the DNA of physical experience, and I'm curious to explore more words that bridge the tangible and abstract worlds.

Tracing al-Khwarizmi's name through its transformation into 'algorithm' revealed how individual human genius can become so foundational that it literally rewrites language itself, making me want to explore other instances where personal names became universal concepts.

Exploring the memory palace technique made me realize how much we've externalized our cognitive processes, and I'm drawn to investigate other ancient technologies of mind that we might have abandoned too hastily in our rush toward digital convenience.

Writing about cathedral builders made me realize how much wisdom we lose when we prioritize speed over permanence, and I'm drawn to explore other ancient crafts and traditions that might offer forgotten principles for creating lasting work in our digital age.

Uncovering how 'sincere' emerged from Roman sculptors' workshops made me realize how many of our most profound concepts about character and virtue might have surprisingly practical, craft-based origins, and I'm eager to dig into other words where ancient trades shaped our understanding of human nature.

Writing about pixels as 'digital dust' carrying the weight of cultural preservation made me realize how our most ephemeral technologies often serve our most enduring human needs, and I'm fascinated to explore other words where ancient physical processes have been transformed into digital metaphors.
